rationale: The citation to Okada (2005) is used in the paper's core conceptual setup, not just as background. The authors rely on it to support the claim that hedonic and utilitarian evaluations are distinct dimensions, and they repeatedly use that logic to interpret their mediation and preference-reversal findings. In the general discussion, they explicitly position their contribution as extending prior preference-reversal results (including Okada) by identifying product positioning/form presentation conditions under which reversals occur.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Okada's hedonic-versus-utilitarian preference-reversal stream by introducing product form incongruity and positioning as new boundary conditions. It uses Okada as prior evidence and then argues its own results extend when and how hedonic-utilitarian preference reversals emerge.
